title: Global: Health and Safety Fundamentals
subtitle: ISO 45001 and ILO Standards for Construction Workers
description: International health and safety principles for civil construction. Based on ISO 45001:2018 and ILO C167. Essential for workers moving between countries or working on international projects.

lessons.title: What is ISO 45001:2018?
lessons.content:
**ISO 45001:2018 — Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems**

ISO 45001 is the world's first international standard for occupational health and safety management systems. Published March 2018, it replaced OHSAS 18001. Adopted in over 130 countries.

**The 10-Clause Structure:**
1. Scope
2. Normative references
3. Terms and definitions
4. Context of the organisation
5. Leadership and worker participation
6. Planning
7. Support
8. Operation
9. Performance evaluation
10. Improvement

**The PDCA Cycle at the Core:**
Plan-Do-Check-Act underpins the entire system:
- PLAN: Identify hazards, set OHS objectives
- DO: Implement controls and processes
- CHECK: Monitor and measure performance
- ACT: Review results and continually improve

**Hierarchy of Controls (Clause 8):**
1. Elimination — remove the hazard entirely (most effective)
2. Substitution — replace with something less hazardous
3. Engineering controls — isolate people from the hazard
4. Administrative controls — change work practices and procedures
5. PPE — protect the individual (least effective — last resort)

lessons.title: ISO 45001 Worker Rights and Participation
lessons.content:
**Clause 5 — Leadership and Worker Participation**

ISO 45001 places strong emphasis on worker participation — a key shift from older standards.

**Worker Rights Under ISO 45001:**
- Right to report hazards and near misses without fear of reprisal
- Right to participate in OHS consultation and decision-making
- Right to withdraw from work situations posing imminent and serious danger
- Right to receive relevant OHS information and training free of charge

**Hazard Identification (Clause 6) — Must Consider:**
- Routine and non-routine activities
- Human factors: fatigue, stress, distraction
- Infrastructure, equipment, and materials
- Design of work areas and systems
- Past incidents and near misses
- Emergency situations

**Continual Improvement (Clause 10):**
ISO 45001 organisations must demonstrate ongoing improvement in OHS performance, not just compliance. Every incident and near miss is an improvement opportunity.

questions.q: ISO 45001:2018 replaced which previous standard?
The PDCA cycle stands for:
In the Hierarchy of Controls, PPE is:
options: The first and most preferred control

questions.q: ISO 45001 gives workers the right to:
options: Withdraw from work posing imminent danger

lessons.title: ILO Convention C167 — Safety in Construction
lessons.content:
**ILO Convention C167 — Safety and Health in Construction (1988)**

The International Labour Organization (ILO) is a United Nations agency setting international labour standards. C167 is the key ILO treaty for construction safety.

**Key ILO C167 Provisions:**

**1. Safe Place of Work**
All workplaces must be kept safe. Access and egress must be safe at all times.

**2. Scaffolding**
Must be erected, used, and dismantled only under supervision of a competent person.

**3. Lifting Appliances and Gear**
Cranes and lifting equipment must be inspected by a competent person. Load limits must never be exceeded.

**4. Excavations**
All excavations must be inspected before each shift by a competent person. Adequate shoring required.

**5. Demolition**
Must be carried out under supervision of a competent person with a plan.

**6. PPE — Free of Charge**
Employers must provide appropriate PPE at no cost to workers where risks cannot otherwise be controlled.

**7. Stop Work Authority**
Workers have the right to withdraw from work situations posing imminent danger — in every country, under ILO principles.

lessons.title: Universal Safety Principles — Every Country
lessons.content:
**Principles That Apply in Every Country**

**1. Call Before You Dig — Always**
- New Zealand: 0800 DIG KNOW
- Australia: 1100
- USA: 811
- Canada: BC 1 Call | Ontario One Call | Alberta One Call

**2. Competent Person for High Risk Work**
Every country requires a competent or qualified person to supervise:
- Excavations and trenching
- Crane and lifting operations
- Scaffolding erection
- Working at heights

**3. Pre-Task Hazard Assessment**
Before any task begins: Identify hazards, assess risk, implement controls, brief all workers.
Called different names by country:
- NZ/AU: JSA (Job Safety Analysis) or SWMS
- USA: JHA (Job Hazard Analysis)
- Canada: JSA or Safe Work Procedure

**4. Incident Reporting Culture**
Report ALL near misses, not just injuries. Near misses are free lessons.

**5. Stop Work Authority**
Every worker in every country has the right to stop work they believe is unsafe. This is recognised by ILO, ISO 45001, and every national OHS system.

lessons.title: Fall Protection Heights by Country
lessons.content:
**Fall Protection Trigger Heights — International Comparison**

This is one of the most important differences when working internationally.

- New Zealand: 2 metres
- Australia: 2 metres
- Canada: 3 metres (most provinces)
- USA: 6 feet (1.8 metres)
- UK and Europe: 2 metres

**Key Message:** When crossing borders, ALWAYS check the local fall protection height — do not assume your home country rules apply.

**Excavator Licensing — International Comparison:**

- New Zealand: No national licence — employer must verify competency
- Australia: No HRW licence required — competency must be demonstrated
- USA: No federal licence — Competent Person standard applies
- Canada: Red Seal HEO voluntary — competency required
- UK: CPCS card widely required on most sites

**Universal Rule:** No matter what country you are in, the employer must ensure you are trained and competent for the specific plant you are operating.

lessons.title: PPE Standards by Country
lessons.content:
**PPE Standards — International Comparison**

**Hard Hats:**
- NZ and AU: AS/NZS 1801
- USA: ANSI/ISEA Z89.1
- Canada: CSA Z94.1
- Europe: EN 397

**High-Visibility Clothing:**
- NZ and AU: AS/NZS 4602.1 — Class D (day) and Class N (night)
- USA: ANSI 107 — Class 1, 2, and 3
- Canada: CSA Z96 — Class 1, 2, and 3
- International: EN ISO 20471

**Safety Footwear:**
- NZ and AU: AS/NZS 2210.3
- USA: ASTM F2413
- Canada: CSA Grade 1

**Silica Dust — Respiratory Protection:**
All countries require minimum P2/N95 respirator for dry concrete or rock cutting.

**Universal Rule:** When working in a new country, always ask which standard applies locally and ensure your PPE is locally compliant before starting work.

**Qualification Recognition:**
- NZ and Australia: Trans-Tasman Mutual Recognition Arrangement (TTMRA) — mutual recognition
- NZ/AU to North America: No automatic recognition — verify local requirements
- Canada Red Seal: Valid across Canadian provinces only

lessons.title: Universal Risk Assessment Process
lessons.content:
**Risk Assessment — Universal 5-Step Process**

1. IDENTIFY the hazard — what could cause harm?
2. IDENTIFY who might be harmed and how
3. EVALUATE the risk — likelihood multiplied by consequence
4. IMPLEMENT controls — apply the hierarchy
5. REVIEW and update regularly

**Risk = Likelihood x Consequence**

- Low risk (1-4): Manage with routine procedures
- Medium risk (5-9): Specific controls and monitoring required
- High risk (10-16): Stop work — significant controls required
- Extreme risk (17-25): Do not proceed without engineering controls and senior approval

**Emergency Numbers by Country:**
- New Zealand: 111
- Australia: 000
- USA: 911
- Canada: 911
- UK: 999

**Universal Emergency Steps:**
1. Ensure safety of everyone — do not create additional casualties
2. Call emergency services
3. Administer first aid if trained
4. Secure the scene — do not disturb evidence
5. Notify supervisor and company
6. Complete incident report
7. Support the investigation

options: Do not proceed without engineering controls and senior approval

After any incident, the FIRST step is:
options: Ensure safety of everyone — do not create more casualties

lessons.title: Course Summary
lessons.content:
**Congratulations — You have completed Global: Health and Safety Fundamentals!**

Key knowledge covered:

- ISO 45001:2018: The global OHS management system standard — 10 clauses, PDCA cycle
- Hierarchy of Controls: Elimination is best, PPE is last resort
- ILO C167: International construction safety convention — PPE free, competent person required
- Stop Work Authority: Every worker in every country has this right
- Fall protection heights vary: NZ/AU 2m, Canada 3m, USA 6ft
- Call Before You Dig varies by country — always call before excavating
- PPE standards vary by country — verify local compliance
- TTMRA: NZ and AU mutually recognise qualifications
- Risk = Likelihood x Consequence
- Report ALL near misses — they are free lessons
